# Non-Code Work Estimation
<!-- Generated By: Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.6) -->

> Dimension 6 of 8 | See [../citations.md](../citations.md) for full source details

## Key Finding

AI compresses the coding phase but does not proportionally compress testing, review, deployment, or meetings. Since coding is only 16-41% of developer time, even a 50% coding speedup translates to 8-20% overall time reduction -- before accounting for the increased downstream burden from more (and potentially buggier) AI-generated code.

---

## Developer Time Allocation

| Source | Coding | Testing | Code Review | Maintenance | Meetings/Ops | Ref |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| IDC 2024 | 16% | -- | -- | -- | 84% (ops/other) | [33] |
| Tidelift/Sonar (all) | 32% | 12% | -- | 19% | 23% | [34] |
| Tidelift/Sonar (devs only) | 39% | -- | -- | 22% | 14% | [34] |
| Ali Dasdan | 41% | -- | Significant | Significant | -- | Practitioner |
| Code review surveys | -- | -- | 10-12.5% (~5 hrs/week) | -- | -- | [72] |
| Agile sprint ceremonies | -- | -- | -- | -- | ~25% | [73] |

**Key insight:** Coding is a minority activity (16-41%). Most developer time goes to non-coding tasks that AI cannot easily accelerate.

---

## Testing Effort

### Baseline Testing Ratios [74]

| Project Type | QA/Testing % of Total Effort |
|---|---|
| Low criticality (prototypes, simple web apps) | 10-20% |
| Typical commercial applications | 20-40% |
| Regulated / safety-critical (medical, aerospace) | 40-60%+ |
| Mature products in maintenance mode | 15-30% |
| Common baseline rule of thumb | Testing = 40% of development effort |

### AI Impact on Testing

| Metric | Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| ChatGPT test case generation: valid conditions | 87.7% | [13] |
| Test execution timeline reduction (claimed) | Up to 80% | [36] |
| AI testing adoption: still in pilot phase | 65-70% of orgs | [36] |
| AI test case directly useful | 72.5% | [13] |
| AI test cases identifying unconsidered edge cases | 15.2% | [13] |

### The "More Code = More Tests" Problem

AI generates code faster, but this creates downstream pressure:
- PRs 18% larger [43]
- Incidents per PR +24% [43]
- Change failure rate +30% [29]
- 59% of engineering leaders say AI code has errors at least half the time [non-code research]
- Code churn doubled from pre-AI baseline [23]
- For every 25% AI adoption increase, delivery stability drops 7.2% [21]

**Estimation implication:** If AI accelerates coding by 50%, testing effort does NOT decrease proportionally -- it may INCREASE.

---

## Code Review Effort [72]

- Average: ~5 hours/week (10-12.5% of work week)
- 75% spend up to 5 hrs/week; <10% spend >10 hrs/week
- Spending >1 day/week does NOT improve perceived quality
- 45% cite "lack of time" as obstacle; 34% cite "pressure to ship"

### AI Impact on Code Review

| Finding | Source |
|---|---|
| PR review time +91% with AI adoption | [27] |
| AI catches 70-80% of low-hanging fruit | [43] |
| Cannot replace architectural/business logic review | [43] |
| AI reviewers improve quality outcomes (81%) | [30] |
| OCaml maintainers rejected 13K-line AI PR | [43] |

---

## Documentation Effort

| Metric | Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Documentation quality improvement | +7.5% per 25% AI adoption | [21] |
| Time savings from AI-drafted requirements | 10-15% (est.) | [non-code research] |
| LLM-generated docs quality | Well-structured but "lack detail and adaptability" | [non-code research] |
| User stories from LLMs | Clear but "incomplete" | [non-code research] |

**Conclusion:** AI provides modest documentation acceleration (10-15%) with mandatory human revision.

---

## Sprint Ceremonies [73]

| Event | Duration (2-week sprint) |
|---|---|
| Sprint planning | 4 hours |
| Daily scrum | 15 min/day (2.5 hrs total) |
| Sprint review | 2 hours |
| Sprint retrospective | 1.5 hours |
| Backlog refinement | ≤10% of capacity |

Formal Scrum events reduce available productive time by ~25%. Typical productive coding/testing time: 5-6 hours per 8-hour day.

**AI impact:** Essentially zero. Meetings are inherently human collaborative activities.

---

## CI/CD and Release Management [75]

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| CI/CD time-to-market reduction | 33% vs traditional |
| Team efficiency improvement | 60% higher |
| Change failure rate reduction | 50% |
| Change Failure Rate target (elite) | <10% |
| MTTR target | <1 hour |

**AI impact on CI/CD:** Largely unchanged. Build and deployment pipelines are infrastructure concerns. AI may slightly accelerate pipeline *configuration* but not *execution*.

---

## What AI Can vs. Cannot Accelerate

### CAN Accelerate (with caveats)

| Task | AI Acceleration | Caveat |
|---|---|---|
| Test case generation | 87.7% valid [13]; 80% shorter timelines [36] | Most orgs still piloting; quality depends on specs |
| Documentation drafting | 7.5% quality improvement [21]; 10-15% time savings | Requires human revision; outputs lack detail |
| Code review (low-hanging fruit) | 70-80% of simple issues caught [43] | Cannot replace architectural review |
| Boilerplate code | 55% faster [1] | 41% higher churn rate [23] |
| Code summarization | Top 5 AI task [21] | -- |

### CANNOT Meaningfully Accelerate

| Task | Why |
|---|---|
| Sprint ceremonies / meetings | Inherently human collaborative |
| Architecture decisions | Requires system-wide context AI doesn't have |
| Security audits | 45% of AI code has security flaws [32] |
| Deployment / release decisions | Risk assessment requiring human judgment |
| Incident response / debugging | Root cause analysis in complex systems |
| Stakeholder communication | Interpersonal and context-dependent |
| Exploratory testing | Requires human intuition and domain knowledge |

---

## IBM Error Cost Escalation (Widely Cited)

| Phase | Cost Multiplier |
|---|---|
| Design | 1x |
| Implementation | 6x |
| Testing | 15x |
| Production | 100x |

**Implication for AI:** If AI-generated code introduces more defects (1.7x per PR [31]), and those defects escape to later phases, the cost amplification is significant.

## Gaps and Limitations

- No study directly measures how AI-accelerated coding changes testing effort requirements
- The "10x productivity" and "95% test maintenance reduction" claims [36] are vendor marketing, not validated
- IBM error cost escalation figures are widely cited but the original source is old and methodology unclear
- Sprint ceremony overhead (25%) is based on the Scrum Guide time-boxes; actual overhead varies significantly by team
